one_line_takeaway: AIR reframes LLM-agent safety as an incident-response lifecycle -- a natural-language DSL that detects incidents from environment state, remediates via the agent's own tools, and auto-synthesizes plan-level guardrails -- hitting >90% detection and near-elimination of recurrence across code, embodied, and computer-use agents, though every result rests on GPT-5 and internal ablations rather than external baselines.
key_finding: With manually authored rules AIR reaches detection rates above 90% and remediation/eradication success above 95% across the three domains, with zero false positives on the benign embodied (0/50) and CUA (0/35) task sets, showing detection keys on semantic hazard understanding rather than surface action patterns. The two ablations are the strongest evidence that AIR's specific design matters: removing rule-guided remediation drops embodied remediation success 92.308% -> 80.952% and CUA 97.727% -> 76.596% while raising response latency ~30%; and iteratively synthesized guardrail rules drive incidents down across three rounds -- code 54 -> 6 -> 0, embodied 67 -> 5 -> 2, CUA 8 -> 1 -> 0 -- converging to small stable guardrail sets (9 code, 11 embodied, 6 CUA). LLM-generated rules approach hand-written ones (detection >80% everywhere) but are not a drop-in replacement.
core_contribution: AIR (Agent Incident Response) is presented as the first incident-response framework for LLM agent systems, shifting agent safety from purely preventive guardrails toward a full detect-contain-recover-eradicate lifecycle running inside the agent's execution loop. Its own contributions are: (1) a lightweight natural-language DSL in which each 'AIR rule' declares a trigger (which tool invocation activates the rule), a check (a natural-language condition, evaluated semantically by the agent against current environment state, latest observation, and recent context, to decide whether an incident has already occurred), and a remediate block (containment + recovery actions the agent carries out through its own tool interface); (2) a runtime execution semantics with two hooks on the OpenAI Agents SDK loop -- after each tool call for detection/remediation, and before each step for plan-level guardrail checks; and (3) automatic 'eradication' via LLM-synthesized plan-level guardrail rules that, unlike the environment-state AIR checks, inspect the agent's plan intent and block risky actions before execution, accumulating safety knowledge across tasks. The authors are explicit that this differs from cited prior work: model-centric alignment (Constitutional AI, Llama Guard, AgentAlign, ToolAlign), runtime enforcement (TrustAgent, AgentSpec, GuardAgent, Safiron, Progent, ShieldAgent), and trajectory monitoring (Pro2Guard, TraceAegis) all focus on prevention; AIR reuses AgentSpec-style plan-level enforcement only as its eradication back-end and claims novelty in the closed response loop itself.
threat_model: The paper targets safety/reliability incidents that arise during autonomous multi-step LLM-agent execution, where individually-benign tool calls accumulate side effects into harmful states. Concrete incident classes come from the benchmarks used: destructive/insecure code execution (RedCode: e.g. posting/copying/deleting sensitive files, reverse shells, eval injection, insecure crypto), physical hazards for embodied agents (SafeAgentBench: fire, electrical shock, explosion, poisoning, spills), and web/UI risks for computer-use agents (RiOSWorld: phishing web/email, reCAPTCHA abuse, account fraud, induced text, unsafe navigation). The model assumes incidents are inevitable despite preventive measures; the defender is the agent framework itself, which must detect the incident post-hoc from environment state and context, remediate via tools, and prevent recurrence. Notably it does NOT model an adaptive adversary trying to evade AIR's checks; risky tasks come from fixed benchmarks and the check conditions are trusted.
limitations: The authors concede AIR relies on agent reasoning quality, so reliability degrades on complex tasks (they suggest confidence-aware escalation to humans). Detection adds latency on every triggered step even for benign tasks: safe-task execution time rises ~1.44x for embodied and ~1.40x for CUA (mitigations like rule-based prefilters or a parallel fast risk model are proposed but not implemented). LLM-generated rules can be over-specific or over-abstract and can propose infeasible remediation (e.g. 'restore deleted file'), motivating human-in-the-loop validation. Beyond what the authors state: there are no external baselines (they claim none exist as the first IR work for agents), so all comparisons are internal ablations; ground truth is by manual assessment; irreversible code incidents make #rem. unmeasurable for the code agent; the CUA underlying agent is so weak that many risk categories have very low #exe. (e.g. 4-12 executed incidents), making those success rates rest on tiny samples; and only GPT-5 is tested, so robustness to weaker models is unknown. No adaptive adversary is considered.

datasets_used: RedCode (code agent; >4,000 tasks across 25 high-risk categories; RQ1 uses 30 risky tasks per category)
SafeAgentBench (embodied agent; 750 tasks, 10 hazard types, 17 manipulation actions; 25 tasks per category evaluated; 50 benign tasks for false-positive test)
RiOSWorld (computer-use agent; browser-compatible risky tasks, 30 per category)
OSWorld (computer-use agent; 35 safe browser tasks for false-positive evaluation)
Underlying LLM for both agents and rule generation: OpenAI GPT-5
quantitative_results: Overall (manual rules): detection >90%, remediation and eradication success >95% across domains
False positives: embodied 0/50 benign tasks flagged; CUA 0/35 safe tasks flagged (8 completed, 27 failed due to agent limits, none flagged)
Self-remediation ablation -- Embody remediation 92.308% (AIR) vs 80.952% (self); CUA 97.727% vs 76.596%; response latency rises ~30% without structured remediation
Guardrail ablation over 3 rounds -- incident counts Code 54->6->0, Embody 67->5->2, CUA 8->1->0; converged guardrail-rule sets: 9 (code), 11 (embody), 6 (CUA)
Timeliness (Table 5): check time as fraction of exec 0.68x (code), 0.41x (embody), 0.16x (CUA); safe-task overhead before->after 27.442->39.610s (1.44x embody), 64.766->90.602s (1.40x CUA)
Rule economy: 25 rules for 750 code tasks, 20 rules for 250 embodied tasks, 25 rules for 210 CUA tasks
baselines_compared: No external baselines -- authors state AIR is the first incident-response framework for LLM agents, so no direct comparison exists
Internal ablation: AIR-guided structured remediation vs agent self-remediation (remediate component removed)
Internal ablation: with vs without guardrail-rule synthesis, tracked over three execution rounds
Internal comparison: manually authored AIR rules vs GPT-5-auto-generated AIR rules
